FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 12, 2007

Mr. Robert Stadum
President Board of Directors
Hi-Desert Water District
225 S. Civic Dr., Ste 1-5
Palm Springs, California 92262-7226

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-012

Dear Mr. Stadum:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in a governmental decision by the Hi-Desert Water District to build, own, operate, or maintain package sewage treatment plants or, in the alternative, a regional wastewater treatment plant, if one or more of these plants were to serve property that you wish to develop?

___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, Sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 2

2. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in a
governmental decision where the Hi-Desert Water District would collect a fee or
tax on all water meters to be issued and sold for new developments?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. You have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act and may not
participate in any decision to build and operate package sewage treatment plants
or a regional wastewater treatment plant if there is a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any of your economic interests as discussed herein.

2. Yes. You have a conflict of interest and may not participate in the decision to
impose a fee on all water meters issued to new developers due to your interests in
real property.

FACTS

You serve as an elected member of the Board of Directors of the Hi-Desert Water
District (the "District") in the capacity of Board President. The District was formed in
1962 as a special district pursuant to California Water Code sections 3000 et seq. and
provides water services to 25,000 customers within a fifty-seven mile radius that includes
the Town of Yucca Valley, as well as an unincorporated area within the County of San
Bernardino.

You own various real property and business interests within the service area of
the District. You are a 50 percent owner in Living Space, a real estate investment and
home building company. Living Space recently purchased a twenty-acre parcel and has
an option to purchase an additional forty acres within the service area of the District.
Following the acquisition of the property, including the option, Living Spaces formed
Yucca Valley 98 Homes ("YV98H"). You own a 25 percent interest in YV98H. At
some future date, YV98H plans to develop the sixty-acre parcel with residential
dwellings.

Currently the District is considering two proposals: (1) the finance and
construction of a regional wastewater treatment plant and possibly the interim use of
package sewage treatment plants; and (2) the imposition of additional water meter fees or
surcharges on new developments paid for by the new developers.

Package Sewage Treatment Plants

Earlier this year, the California Regional Water Quality Control Board (the
"Regional Board") advised all local developers that it will prohibit all standard septic
tanks because of the risks those tanks pose to underground water supplies. The Regional
Board now requires enhanced types of treatment for all new developments (excluding in-
fill). As a result, YV98H retained a consultant to advise it on wastewater treatment
alternatives. The consultant recommended that YV98H consider developing a package

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 3

sewage treatment plant to serve its development. The consultant recommended this
option to the District and, in addition, suggested that the District consider either: (1)
building such facilities for new developments at the developer's expense; or (2)
considering operating, maintaining, and repairing the package sewage treatment plants
once they are built. Currently the District does not provide wastewater treatment, nor
does it maintain, operate, or repair package sewage treatment plants. District staff
recommended against the District maintaining, operating, and repairing package sewage
treatment plants, and no action has been taken one way or another by the Board on the
issue of package sewage treatment plants.

The District continues to consider the financing, construction, operation, and
maintenance of a large regional wastewater treatment plant, which could serve larger
portions of the District's entire service area. The District previously acquired a site for
the location of its regional treatment plant.

Meter Surcharge on New Developments

At a recent board meeting the District considered an informal proposal to impose
a water meter surcharge on all new developments as a way to pay for the additional water
resources that will be needed to provide future water supplies. Under the proposal, new
developers would pay a surcharge for a water meter in addition to charges associated with
the purchase of such a water meter. Because you plan to develop property located in the
boundaries of the District, you would be a new developer and thus subject to the new
water meter fees.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In order to determine whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analytical framework. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-
(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 4

Step One: Are You a Public Official?

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act only apply to public officials.
(Section 87100.) Public officials include members of committees, boards or
commissions with decision making authority. (Regulation 18701(a)(1).) Officials of all
special purpose districts are also included under the provisions of the rule. As a member
of the Hi-Desert Water District you are a public official under the Act.

Step Two: Will You be Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a
Governmental Decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) As a boardmember of
the District you will be making a governmental decision if you vote on either the
construction of the waste water treatment plant or the water meter fee on all new
developers.

In addition, a public official "participates in making" a governmental decision
when he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes
recommendations regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) Therefore, you will be
participating in making a governmental decision if you advise, recommend or negotiate a
course of action for the District pertaining to the construction of the treatment plants or
imposition of a water fee on new developers.²

Furthermore, a public official is "influencing a governmental decision" if he or
she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of the District regarding the decisions. (Regulation 18702.3)

Step Three: What Are Your Economic Interests?

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)), or in which

² Exception: Making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly
owned real property or business entity. (See regulation 18702.4) This exception does not apply to you as
you are a joint owner of both Living Space and YV98H.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 5

he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also
knows as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

For the purposes of this section indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity in which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

Economic Interests Identified

Based on the facts submitted, you have identified the following economic
interests:

Business Entities:

You have an economic interest in the business entity Living Space both because
you have an investment of $2,000 or more and because you are a partner in the business.
Additionally, you have an economic interest in the business entity YV98H because you
indicated that your 25 percent ownership interest in YV98H represents an investment of
$2,000 or more. If you are an officer, director, partner, employee, or hold any position of
management in YV98H you would also have an economic interest in the company based
on your position. (See Regulation 18703.1(b).)

Moreover, the Act provides that an indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) Because you
own a 25 percent interest in YV98H and a 50-percent interest in Living Space, you also
have an interest in any investment or interest owned by either of these two business
entities.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 6

Interest in Real Property:

Interest in real property of an individual includes a "pro rata share of interests in
real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual…owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 82033.) Because
you own a 25-percent interest in Living Space and a 50-percent interest in YV98H, you
have an economic interest in the real property held by those business entities. This
includes not only the twenty-acre parcel owned outright by Living Spaces but also the
forty acres in which living spaces has an option to purchase. An option to purchase
property constitutes an interest in that property for purposes of Section 87103(b). (Heidt
Advice Letter, No. A-90-245).

Sources of Income:

An official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received income aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time when the
relevant decision is made. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3(a)(1).) Additionally,
income of an individual includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-
percent interest or greater. Accordingly, in addition to the economic interests in your
business entities as a source of income to you, you also have an economic interest in any
individual who provided income to the businesses if your pro rata share of the income
equals $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.

You have not provided any facts indicating the amount of income you may have
received from any source. For purposes of our analysis, we will assume you have a
source of income economic interest in each of your businesses. You have not indicated
any other sources of income, including a pro rata share of the business income from any
customers of the business.³ Additionally, you have not provided any information
regarding other economic interests you may have. Accordingly, our analysis is limited to
your economic interest in your identified business entities, including the real property
holding of those businesses.

Step Four: Are Your Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?

Business Entity, Source of Income:

A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, are
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or indirectly or by an agent:

³ Please note that should you sell a home or property related to the development, from which you
receive $500 or more within 12 months of any governmental decision you will need to determine whether
any of the buyers qualify as a source of income and whether that may lead to a potential conflict of interest
in the decision.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 7

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person." (Section 87103; Regulation 18704.1(a).)

If the official's interest in a business entity, source of income or source of a gift is
not directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, they are deemed to be
indirectly involved. (See Cooke Advice Letter, No. A-99-127.)

Decision One: Sewage Treatment Facilities

You state that because of a new prohibition against all standard septic tanks, the
Regional Board now requires "enhanced types of treatment for all new developments."
You have not mentioned any specific governmental decision facing the District other
than to state that the District "continues to consider the financing, construction, operation
and maintenance of a large regional wastewater treatment plant, which would serve larger
portions of the District's entire service area" and that a "site for the location of its
regional wastewater treatment plant has been previously acquired by the District."

At this stage it appears the decisions are general in nature and involve exploring
whether to implement a package water treatment facilities plan or, in the alternative, to
proceed with a larger regional wastewater treatment plant in order to meet the need of
any new developments. Assuming your business did not initiate the proceeding, and is
not a named party in or a subject of the proceeding as outlined above, for purposes of our
analysis, it will be indirectly involved. Without a specific governmental decision, you
have not met sufficient information for us to make a determination in this regard.
Once the governmental decision has been formalized, you should be able to make this
determination as outlined above.⁴

Decision Two: Meter Surcharge on New Developments

You state that there has been an informal proposal by one or more members of the
District to consider charging a water meter "surcharge" for each new home to be
provided only by the District. Again, although the outcome of this decision will have an
impact on businesses engaged as developers for any new homes in the district, because

⁴ For example, neither of your business entities would be directly involved if the decision is to
select either package treatments plants or the larger regional plant for new developments. However, if the
decision was to allow either of your businesses, specifically, to construct a package sewage treatment plant
as proposed by your consultant for any new development it initiates, or if it were to authorize the
construction of a specific package water treatment plant in conjunction with the approval of one of your
developments, your business entity would be directly involved in the decision.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 8

no specific entity has initiated the proceeding or is involved as a named party or subject
of the proceeding, your businesses are indirectly involved.

Interest in Real Property:

Decision One: Sewage Treatment Facilities

Regulation 18704.2(a) provides that real property is directly involved in a
decision when the real property in which the official has an interest is "located within 500
feet" of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property that is the subject of
the governmental decision. If the board decides to build its facilities within a 500-foot
radius of property in which any of your companies has an interest (discussed above), your
property would be directly involved in that decision.

Moreover, real property is directly involved in a decision when the decision
involves "construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or
similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive
new or improved services." (Regulation 18704.2(a)(6).) Accordingly, if your properties
would receive new water or sewer services as a result of the package sewage treatment
proposal or through the construction of a regional wastewater treatment plant, your
property would be directly involved in the decision.

Decision Two: Water Meter Fee

The Commission regulations also specify that an official's property interest is
directly involved in a governmental decision when the property is the subject of a
decision that would levy taxes or fees on the property. (See regulation 18704.2(a)(4).)
Following this rule, if the District were to vote on a proposal that would impose a fee on
all water meters installed on all new developments, which includes your property, your
property would be directly involved in this governmental decision.

If the real property is not directly involved in either of these decisions, it is
deemed to be indirectly involved. (Cooke Advice Letter, No. A-99-127.)

Step Five: Applicable Materiality Standard.

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Section
87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest is material.

Business Entity, Source of Income:

The financial effect of a governmental decision on a business entity that is
indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed to be material only if
certain thresholds are met. Because we have determined that your business interests are

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 9

indirectly involved in the decision to build the waste water treatment plant, the
Commission must apply different materiality tests, depending upon the nature of the
economic interests involved.

You have not provided detailed information about the finances for YV98H or
Living Space, but for business entities that are not directly involved in a governmental
decision the materiality standard provided in Regulation 18705.1(c) will apply. These
materiality standards are based on the size of the business.

We do not have details on the size of your businesses and therefore cannot
provide advice on the specific materiality standard that would apply. We enclose a copy
of Regulation 18705.1 to assist you in making this determination. Note that the same
materiality standards referenced in Regulation 18705.1 are also used for sources of
income that are business entities. (Regulation 18705.3(b)(1).)

Interest in Real Property:

Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property that is directly involved in the governmental decision is
presumed to be material. Pursuant to the regulation, this presumption may be rebutted by
proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any
financial effect, not even a "penny's worth," on the real property. This is known as the
"one penny rule."

Regulation 18705.1(b)(1) states that financial effect of a governmental decision
on real property that is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not
to be material. This presumption can be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, that make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest.

Should the District consider a decision to finance or construct a regional
wastewater treatment plant or use package treatment facilities, and the decision directly
involves your property as set forth under Regulation 18704.2(b), the financial effect on
your interest in the real property would be presumed to be material. Similarly, the
financial effect on your property would be presumed material in any decision to impose a
water meter fee on all new developments. The presumption of materiality applies in
these situations, as it is clear that there will be at least a "one penny" financial effect on
your property.

However, if the decision relates to package treatment facilities not directly
involving your properties under the standards set forth above, the financial effects on
your property would be presumed to not be material, although the presumption could be
rebutted as discussed above.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 10

Step Six: Is it Reasonably Foreseeable That the Financial Effect of the
Governmental Decision Upon Your Economic Interests Will Meet the Applicable
Materiality Standard?

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder
of fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Business Entity, Source of Income:

Because we do not have sufficient facts to determine what materiality standard
applies to your business interests, we cannot conclude that it is or is not reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the two decisions before the District will meet the
applicable materiality standard. Should you determine that your business will be
materially affected by the governmental decision as set forth in regulation
18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(B), you will then need to determine if the effect is reasonably
foreseeable.

However, please be aware that if it is reasonably foreseeable that any single
economic interest identified by a public official is materially affected by a governmental
decision, the governmental decision will be presumed to have a material effect on the
public official. This is the case even if it has been determined that the effect of a
governmental decision on several other economic interests will not be material.

Interest in Real Property:

As discussed above, if the District decisions to finance or construct a regional
wastewater plant, use package treatment facilities or impose a water meter fee on all new
developments directly involve your property, the financial effect of any of these decisions
is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) As a consequence, it follows
that the financial effect of any of one of those decisions on your property will be
reasonably foreseeable. Thus, unless an exception applies as discussed in Steps Seven
and Eight below, you will have a disqualifying conflict of interest and cannot vote on,
participate in or otherwise try to influence these government decisions. However, if one
of the District decisions does not directly involve your property, such as a decision
relating to the use of a package treatment facility on property that is located more than
500 feet from and does not otherwise have an impact on your property (see Regulation
18705.2(b)(1)), you will not have a disqualifying conflict of interest in that decision.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 11

Step Seven: The Public Generally Exception.

Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to participate in the governmental decision in question. If the reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official's economic
interest is indistinguishable "from its effect on the public generally," then the public
official does not have a conflict. (Section 87103; Regulations 18700(b)(7), 18707(a).)

To come under the purview of the public generally exception the decision
affecting the public official's economic interests must also affect a significant segment of
the public generally in substantially the same manner as it affects the public official.
(Regulation 18707.1(b)(1) and (2).) The public generally exception applies if both prongs
of this two-prong test are met. In other words, first the official must determine if the
decision affects a significant segment of the population. If the prong is met, then the
official must determine that the segment is affected in substantially the same manner as
the public official.

In addition to the basic rule for the "public generally" exception found in
regulation 18707.1, there are six specialized forms of the exception provided in
regulations 18707.2 -18707.9, which we will briefly discuss.

Under the facts you have provided, your economic interests are your business
interests in Living Space and YV98H, which include sources of income and real property
economic interests to you.

Business Entity, Source of Income:

Significant Segment: For the basic public generally exception to apply to a public
official's economic interest in a business entity, the decision must also affect either 2,000
or 25 percent of all business entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official
represents. In addition, the affected segment must be from more than just a single
industry, trade, or profession. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(C).)

You have not provided facts to suggest that the decision to build a sewage
treatment facility or sewage treatment plants will affect a significant segment of
the business community nor have you provided information that would suggest that the
proposal will affect more than just a single industry or trade. The same is true with
respect to the decision to impose a fee on new water meters. Accordingly, the first prong
of the public generally exception has not been met.

The rules of the Act provide several specialized forms of the "public generally"
exception. (Regulations 18702.2 – 18707.9.) The rates and assessments rule provides
that the exception is met if the decision is to establish or adjust assessments, taxes, fees,
charges, or rates falls on a proportional basis on the official's economic interest and on a
significant segment of the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18707.2(a).) Because the rate the

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 12

District is considering would apply only to new developers, it is unlikely that the water
meter fee would apply to a significant segment of your jurisdiction.

The decision could also be exempt under the public generally exception if the
decision is made by a governing board of a water, irrigation, or similar district to
establish or adjust assessments, taxes, fees, charges, or rates or other similar decisions
such as the allocations of services applied on a proportional or "across-the-board" basis
on the official's economic interests and ten percent of the property owners or other
persons receiving services form the official's agency. (Regulation 18707.2(c).) Again,
because the fee only applies to new developers, it is unlikely that this exception would apply
unless the facts establish that new developers comprise ten percent of the property
owners in the District's jurisdiction.⁵ Therefore it appears likely the "public generally"
special rule for rates and assessments under Regulation 18707.2(c) does not apply.

Finally, subsection 18707.7 provides a special rule for defining when an industry
trade, or profession constitutes a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction. (Section
18707.1(b)(1)(C).) The single industry special exception provides that where a decision
will affect an industry, trade, or profession in substantially the same manner as the
decision will affect an official's economic interest, the industry, trade, or profession
constitutes a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction if that industry is a predominate
industry the official's jurisdiction. "Predominate" under the rule is defined as being 50
percent or more of business entities in the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18707.7(b).)

The purpose of this specialized form of the exception is to "avoid disqualification
in such cases as a farmer elected in a rural community in which agriculture is the major
industry." (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62, n. 3.) In other words, the exception is
limited to a situation where a local economy is based on one industry so that almost any
public official would have an economic tie to that industry. (Woods Advice Letter, No.
A-94-164.)

Under the facts you have provided, it would appear unlikely that the real estate
development industry constitutes 50 percent or more of all the business entities in your
jurisdiction. Therefore it is not the predominate industry within the jurisdiction and the
public generally special exemption for single industry or trades is not applicable.

Interest in Real Property:

Significant Segment: When a public official's economic interest is in real
property, the decision in question must also affect ten percent or more of all property
owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents to
qualify under the public generally exception. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(B)(i).) This
exception also applies if the decision affects 5,000 property owners in the jurisdiction.
(Regulation 18707.1(b)(B)(ii).)

⁵ For purposes of counting property owners, each developer would count as one property owner.
You may not count parcels (Doi Advice Letter, No. I-04-076) nor may you count future property owners of
those parcels, even if the water meter feeds are passed along to those buyers.

File No. A-07-012
Page No. 13

Should the District decide to impose the water meter fee, the fee would be charged to new developers. It is unlikely that new developers would comprise ten percent of all property owners or, in the alternative, 5,000 property owners. With respect to the District's decision regarding package sewage treatment facilities or, in the alternative, building a regional sewage treatment facility, you have not presented sufficient information for us to determine if the decision would affect ten percent of all property owners or 5,000 property owners. However, even if you meet the significant segment test, given the extent of your real property interests it appears that you own considerably more property than those property owners who would fall within the significant segment, and you would not be able to satisfy the second prong of the test because your property would be affected in a substantially different manner than those within the significant segment. Therefore, your real property interests do not fall within the public generally exception.

Please be advised that a determination of the application of the "public generally" exception is factually dependent. Because you have given only a general nature of the possible decisions regarding the sewage treatment options, we are only able to provide you with general guidance in this area. At this time we cannot advise you as to whether the "public generally" exception would apply with respect to all future possibilities. Please feel free to seek further advice should you obtain more information concerning any specific governmental decision regarding plans the District may have for wastewater treatment facilities in your jurisdiction. 6

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Melissa Scolari
Legal Intern, Legal Division

6 The Eighth Step concerns the legally required participation exception. You stated that you did not believe your participation would be legally required, thus we do not discuss the exception herein.

wastewater treatment plant, if one or more of these plants were to selre property

sections.facts:
You serve as an elected member of the Board of Directors of the Hi-Desert Water
District (the'District') in the capacity of Board President. The District was formed in
1962 as a special district pursuant to California Water Code sections 3000 et seq. and
provides water services to 25,000 customers within a fifty-seven mile radius that includes
the Town of Yucca Yalley, as well as an unincorporated area within the County of San
Bernardino.
You own various real property and business interests within the service area of
the District. You are a 50 percent owner in Living Space, arealestate investrnent and
home building company. Living Space recently purchased a twenty-acre farcet and has
an option to purchase an additional forty acres within the service area of the District.
Following the acquisition of the property, including the option, Living Spaces formed
Yucca valley 98 Homes ("yvggH'). you own a is percentinterest in tvqsH. at
some future date, YV98H plans to develop the sixty-acre parcel with residential
dwellings.
currently the District is considering two proposals: (1) the finance and
construction of a regional wastewater treatment plant and possibly the interim use of
package sewage treatment plants; and (2) the imposition of additional water meter fees or
surcharges on new developments paid for by the new developers.
Packaqe Sewaee Treatment plants
Earlier this year, the California Regional Water Quality Control Board (the
"Regional Board') advised all local developers that it wiit protriuit all standard septic
tanks because of the risks those tanks pose to underground water supplies. The Regional
Board now requires enhanced types of treatment forall new develoiments (excluding in-
filI). As a result, YV98H retained a consultant to advise it on wastewater treatment
alternatives. The consultant recommended that YV98H consider developing u pu"tug.


File No. A_07_012
sewage treatment plant to serve its development. The consultant recommended this
option to the District and, in addition, suggested that the District consider either: (1)
building such facilities for new developments at the developer,s expens e; or (2)
considering operating, maintaining, and repairing the packag" ,"*ug" treatment plants
once they are built. Currently the District does not provide wastewalter treatment, nor
does it maintain, operate, or repair package r"*ug"ir"atment plants. District staff
recorlmended against the District maintaining, operating, andiepairing package sewage
treatment plants, and no action has been taken one rvuy o. another by tte Board on the
issue of package sewage treatment plants.
The District continues to consider the financing, construction, operation, and
maintenance of a large regional wastewater treatment plant, which could serve larger
portions of the District's entire service area. The District previously acquired a site for
the location of its regional treatment plant.
At a recent board meeting the District considered an informal proposal to impose
awater meter surcharge on all new developments as a way to pay ror itre aaaitional water
resources that will be needed to provide future water supplies.- tinder the profosal, new
developers would pay a surcharge for a water meter in addition to charges^ass'ociated with
the purchase of such awater meter. Because you plan to develop propJrty located in the
boundaries of the District, you would be a new developer and thus suij""i to ih".r"*
water meter fees.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused uy ttreir own financial interests
or the financial interest ofpersons who have supported thern. (Section S1001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits anypublii official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u io""*1nJrtut
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeablel'that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official,s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In orderio determine whether a public
official has a disqualifuing conflict of interest ina given govemmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analytical framew-ork. (Regulation 1g700(b)(1).
(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists-whenever a public
official makes a govemmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section g7103.)
Step One: Are You a Public Official?
The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act only apply to public officials.
(Section 87100.) Public officials include members of committees, boards or
commissions with decision making authority. (Regulation 18701(a)(l).) Officials of all
special pulpose districts are also included under the provisions of the rule. As a member
of the Hi-Desert water District you are a public official under the Act.
step Two: wiII You be Making, Participating in Making or rnfluencing a
Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the official , acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L) As a boardmember of
the District you will be making a govemmental decision if you vote on either the
construction of the waste water treatment plant or the water meter fee on all new
developers.
In addition, a public official "participates in making" a governmental decision
when he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes
recorrmendations regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) Therefore, you will be
participating in making a govemmental decision if you advise, reconlmend or negotiate a
course of action for the District pertaining to the construction of the treatment plants or
imposition of a water fee on new developers.2
Furthermore, a public official is "influencing a govemmental decision" if he or
she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any mernber, officer,
employee, or consultant of the District regarding the decisions. (Regul ation 18702.3)
Step Three: What Are Your Economic Interests?
A public official has a "financial interest" in agovernmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; regulations 18703-15703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 1S703.1(a)), or in which
2 Exception: Making orparticipating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the oflicial,s wholly
owned real property or business entity. (See regulation 18702.4) This exception does not apply to you as
you are a joint oumer of both Living Space and YV98H.


a
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 1 03 (d); Regulation I 3703. I (b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 37103(b); Regulation 15703.2);
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her imrnediate family. This is also
knows as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 13703.5.)
For the purposes of this section indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity in which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or benefici ally a I O-percent interest or greater. (Section g7l 03.)
Economic Interests fdentified
Based on the facts submitted, you have identified the following economic
interests:
Business Entities:
You have an economic interest in the business entity Living Space both because
you have an investment of $2,000 or more and because you are a partner in the business.
Additionally, you have an economic interest in the business entityYV9SH because you
indicated that your 25 percent ownership interest in YV98H represents an investment of
$2,000 or more. If you are an officer, director, partner, ernployee, or hold any position of
management in YV98H you would also have an economic interest in the company based
on your position. (See Regulation 18703.1(b).)
Moreover, the Act provides that an indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or benefici ally a 1 0 percent interest or greater. (Section 871 03.) Because you
own a 25 petcent interest in YV98H and a 50-percent interest in Living Space, you also
have an interest in any investment or interest owned by either of these two business
entities.
Interest in Real property:
Interest in real property of an individual includes a "pro rata share of interests in
real propertY of any business entity or trust in which the individual...owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficia[ly, a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section g2033.) Because
you own a25-percent interest in Living Space and a 50-percent interest in yV9gH, you
have an economic interest in the real property held by those business entities. This
includes not only tfrg.twentr-acre parcel owned outright by Living spaces but also the
forty acres in which living spaces has an option to puichase. An option to purchase
property constitutes an interest in that property for purposes of Section 871b3@). (Heidt
Advice Letter, No. 4-90-245).
Sources oflncome:
An official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received income aggregating $500 or more within f2 months prior to the time when the
relevant decision is made. (Section 87103(c); Regulation t azb:.:1a;(1).) Additionally,
income of an indiv. idual includes a pro rata share of u.ry income of any lusiness entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or U"r"n"iully, a l0-
percent interest or greater. Accordingly, in addition to the economic interests i, yo.r,
business entities as a source of income to you, you also have an economic interest in any
individuals who provided income to the businesses if yourpro rata share of the income
equals $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision.
You have not provided any facts indicating the arnount of income you may have
received from any source. For purposes of our analysis, we will assume you have a
source of income economic interest in each of your tusinesses. you have not indicated
any other sources of 
,incomre, iillghg 
a pro rata share of the business income from any
customers of the business. ' Additionally, you have not provided any information
regarding other economic interests you may have. Accordingly, ouianalysis is limited to
your economic interest in your identified business entities, inctuaing the ieal property
holding of those businesses.
Step Four: Are Your Economic fnterests Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?
Business Entity, Source oflncome:
A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, are
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or indirectly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates lhe proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) rs a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the

If the official's interest in a bu-siness entity, source of income or source of a gift is
not directly involved in a decision before the official's agency they are deerned to be
indirectly involved. (see cooke Advice Letter,No. a-oe- t zi.y
You state that because of a new prohibition against all standard septic tanks, the
Regional Board now requires "enhanced tlpes of trea=tment for all new developments.,,
You have not mentioned any specific governmental decision facing the District, other
than to state that the District "continues to consider the financing, Jonstruction, operation
and maintenance of a large regional wastewater treatrnent plant,ihich woulJ serve larger
portions of the District's entire service area" and that a'.siie for the location of its
regional wastewater treatment plant has been previously acquired by the District.,,
At this stage it appears the decisions are general in nature and involve exploring

You state that there has been an informal proposal by one or more members of the

Interest in Real property:
Regulation 18704.2(a) provides that real property is directly involved in a

Moreover, real property is directly involved in a decision when the decision

Decision Two: Water Meter Fee
The Commission regulations also specifu that an official's property interest is

Following this rule, if the District were to_ vote on u proporal tliat would impose a fee on

If the real property is not directly involved in either of these decisions, it is

Step Five: Applicable Materiality Standard.
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govemmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Section
87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether reasonably
foreseeable frnancial effect on an economic interestls material.
Business Entity, Source of Income:
The financial effect of a govemmental decision on a business entity that is
indirectly involved in the goveflrmental decision is presumed to be material only if
certain thresholds are met. Because we have determined that your business interests are


File No. A-07_012
indirectly involved in the decision to build the waste water treatment plant, the
Commission must apply different matenality tests, depending upon the nature of the
economic interests involved.
You have not provided detailed information about the finances for WggH or
Living Space, but for business entities that are not directly involved i., u goreor-"rtut
decision the materiality standard provided in Regulation iazos.l(c) will T*rr.These
materiality standards are based on the size of the business.
we do not have details on the size of your businesses and therefore cannot
provide advice on the specific materiality standard that would apply- We enclose a copy
of Regulation 18705.1 to assist you in making this determination. Note that the same
mateiality standards referenced in Regulatio, t SZOS. t are also used for sources of
income that are business entities. (Reguration r g705.3(bxr).)
Interest in Real property:
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property that is directly involved in the governmental decision is
presumed to be material. Pursuant to the regulation, this presumption may be rebutted by
proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable_that the govemmental decision *ill hur" *y
financial effect, not even a "penny's worth," on thf real property. This is known as the
"one penny rule."
Regulation 18705.1(bxl) states that financial effect of a governmental decision
on real property that is indirectly involved in the govemmental d-ecision is presumed not
to be material. This presumption can be rebuttedlyproof that there "* #"in.
circumstances regarding the goverrmental decisiorl its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property in wlich the public official has an economic interest, ihat make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest.
Should the District consider a decision to finance or construct a regional
wastewater treatment plant oruse package treatment facilities, and the deJsion directly
involves your property as set forth undeiRegulation 1g704.2(b), the financial effect on
your interest in the real property would be presumed to be material. Similarly, the
financial effect on your property would be presumed material in any aecision to impose a
water meter fee on lllnew developments, Thepresumption ofmateriality afpties in
these situations, as it is clear that there will be ai least u"'or" penny, financial effect on
your property.
However, if the decision relates to package treatment facilities not directly
involving your properties under the standards set-forth above, the financial effects on
your property would be presumed to not be material, although the presumption could be
rebutted as discussed above.


File No. A_07_012
Step Six: Is it Reasonably Foreseeable That the Financiat Effect of the
Governmental Decision upon Your Economic rnterests will Meet the Applicable
Materiality Standard?
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govefirmental decision is made depends on the facts of eact iu.ti".rtu. "ur".
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substanlially likely.,, 6"gufuUo, 1g706;
In re Thorner (1975),1 FPPC ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a Iertainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. on the other hand , if aneffect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. Qbid.) Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder
of fact when providing advice. (In re ogresby (rg75) 1 Fppc ops. 71.)
Business Entity, Source of Income:
Because we do not have sufficient facts to determine what materiality standard
applies to your business interests, we cannot conclude that it i. oii. ,oi."uro.ruury
foreseeable that the financial effect of the two decisions before the District will meet the
applicable mateiality standard. Should you determine that your business will be
materially affected by the govemmental decision as set fortti in regulation
18705'1(c)(a)(A)-(B), you will then need to determine if the effecfis reasonably
foreseeable.
However, please be aware that if it is reasonably foreseeable that any single
economic interest identified by a public official is materially affecteduy u jor".rr-"rrrut
decision, the governmental decision will be presumed to have a material effect on the
public official. This is the case even if it has been determined that the effect of a
governmental decision on several other economic interests will notbe material.
Interest in Real property:
As discussed above, if the District decisions to finance or construct a regional
wastewater plant, use package treatment facilities or impose a water meter fee on all new
developments directly involve your property, the financial effect of any of these decisions
is presumed to be material- (Regulation ttios.zlal(l).) As a consequence, it follows
that the financial effect of any of one of those a""isio". on your property will be
reasonably foreseeable. Thus, unless an exception applies as discussed in steps Seven
and Eight below, you will have a disqualifyirg.orni"t of interest and cannot'vote on,
participate in or otherwise try to influence these govefirment decisions. However, if one
of the government decisions does not directly involve your property, such as a decision
relating to the use of a package treatment acifty on property that is located more than
500 feet from and does not otherwise have an impacf oryour property (see Regulation
18705'2(b)(1)), you will not have a disqualifying confliciof interest in that decision.
Step Seven: The Public Generally Exception.
Even if a public official otherwise has a conflict of interest, he or she may still be
able to participate in the govefilmental decision in question. If thoreasonably foreseeable
mateial financial effect of a governmental decision on the public official,s elonomic
interest is indistinguishable "from its effect on the public ginerally,,, then the public
official does not have a conflict. (Section 87103; negulations rSiriO6y 0),lg7o7(a).)
To come under the purview of the public ganerally exception the decision
affecting the public official's economic interests must alsl affeci asignificant segment of
the public generally in substantially the same manner as itaffects the"ffilic official.
(Regulation 18707 -1(b)(1) and (2).) The public generally exception applies if both prongs
of this two-prong test are met. In other words, first ttre ofnciai must detemine if the
decision affects a significant segment of the population. If the prong is met, then the
official must determine that the segment is affected in subsrantiallyThe sam;;e manner as
the public official.
In addition to the basic rule for the "public generally,, exception found in
regulation 18707.1, there are six specialized forms of the exception provided in
regulations 18707.2 -18707.9, which we will briefly discuss.
Under the facts you have provided, your economic interests are your business
interests in Living Space and YV98H, which include sources of income and real property
economic interests to you.
Business Entity, Source of Income:
Sienificant Seernent: For the basic public generally exception to apply to a public
official's economic interest in a business entity, the decision must also affect-eithei2,ggg
or 25 percent of all business entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official
represents- In addition, the affected segment must be from more than just a single
industry, trade, or profession. (Regulation 1 g707. 1 (bXC).)
You have not provided facts to suggest that the decision to build a sewage
treatment facility or package sewage treatment plants will affect a significant segment of
the business community nor have you provided information that worild suggest that the
proposal will affect more than just a single industry or trade. The same islire with
respect to the decision to impose a fee on new water meters. AccordinglS the first prong
of the public generally exception has not be met.
The rules of the Act provide several specialized forms of the "public generally',
exception. (Regulations 18702.2 - 18707.9.) The rates and assessments rule provides
that the exception is met if the decision is to establish or adjust assessments, iaxes, fees,
charges, or rates falls on a proportional basis on the officiai's economic interest and on a
significant segment of the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18707.2(a).) Because the rate the


File No. A_07_012
District is considering would awly only to new developers, it is unlikely that the water
meter fee would apply to a significant segment of yourjurisdiction.
The decision could also be exempt under the public generally exception if the
decision is made by a governing board of a water, irrigation, or simijar disirict to
establish or adjust assessments, taxes, fees, charg"r, oi rates or other similar decisions,
such as the allocations of services applied on a proportional or "across-the-board,, basis
on the official's economic interests and ten percent of the property owners or other
persons receiving services form the official's agency. (Regulation 18707.2(c).) Again,
because the fee only applies to new developers, it isunlikety ttrls exception would ipply
unless the facts establish that new developers comprise ten percent of ihe property
owners in the District's jurisdiction.) Therefore it appears likely the ',public generally,,
special rule for rates and assessments under Regulation 18707.2(c) does not ipply.
Finally, subsection 18707.7 provides a special rule for defining when an industry
trade, or profession constitutes a "significant segment" of the jurisdictLn. (Section
18707.1(bXl)(C).) The single industry special exception provides that wheie a decision
will affect an industry, trade, or profession in substantially the same manner as the
decision will affect an official's economic interest, the industry, trade, or profession
constitutes a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction if that industry is a predominate
industry the official's jurisdiction. '?redominate" under the rule is definei as being 50
percent or more of business entities in the official's jurisdiction. (Regulatio n tn01.161.1
The purpose of this specialized form of the exception is to "avoid disqualification
in such cases as a farmer elected in a rural community in which agriculture is the major
industry." Qn re Ferraro (1975) 4 FPPC Ops. 62, n. 3.) In other words, the exception is
limited to the situation where a local economy is based on one industry so that almost any
public official would have an economic tie to that indusky. (Woods Advice Letter, No.
A-e4-164.)
Under the facts you have provided, it would appear unlikely that the real estate
development industry constitutes 50 percent or more of all the business entities in your
jurisdiction. Therefore it is not the predominate industry within the jurisdiction and the
public generally special exemption for single industry oi t ud", is n& applicable.
Interest in Real Property:
Si8rrificant Seprnent: When a public official's economic interest is in real
property, the decision in question must_also affect ten percent or more of all property
owners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents to
qualiff under the public generally exception. (Regulation ts7o7.1(b)(B)(i).) This
exception also applies if the decision affects 5,000 property owners in the jurisdiction.
(Regulatio n 1 87 07 .1 (b)(Bxii).)
5 For purposes of co-unting properfy ow-lersl each developer would count as one property owner.
You may not countparcels (Doi Advice Letler, No. I-04-076) ,oi *uy you count future properry owners of

Should the District decide to impose the water meter fee, the fee would be

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 April 12, 2007 Mr. Robert Stadum President Board of Directors Hi-Desert Water District 225 S. Civic Dr., Ste 1-5 Palm Springs, California 92262-7226 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-07-012 Dear Mr. Stadum: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in a governmental decision by the Hi-Desert Water District to build, own, operate, or maintain package sewage treatment plants or, in the alternative, a regional wastewater treatment plant, if one or more of these plants were to serve property that you wish to develop? ___________________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, Sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-07-012 Page No. 2 2. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from participating in a governmental decision where the Hi-Desert Water District would collect a fee or tax on all water meters to be issued and sold for new developments? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. You have a prohibited conflict of interest under the Act and may not participate in any decision to build and operate package sewage treatment plants or a regional wastewater treatment plant if there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your economic interests as discussed herein. 2. Yes. You have a conflict of interest and may not participate in the decision to impose a fee on all water meters issued to new developers due to your interests in real property. FACTS You serve as an elected member of the Board of Directors of the Hi-Desert Water District (the "District") in the capacity of Board President. The District was formed in 1962 as a special district pursuant to California Water Code sections 3000 et seq. and provides water services to 25,000 customers within a fifty-seven mile radius that includes the Town of Yucca Valley, as well as an unincorporated area within the County of San Bernardino. You own various real property and business interests within the service area of the District. You are a 50 percent owner in Living Space, a real
